challenges.situation: A customer support team is measured on 'average ticket resolution time.' Over the past quarter, the metric improved dramatically — tickets are being closed 40% faster. But customer satisfaction surveys show increasing frustration. Agents are closing tickets prematurely with 'Did this resolve your issue?' and marking them solved when customers don't respond within 24 hours.

options.text: The agents are gaming the system and need to be disciplined — their behavior is dishonest and undermines the customer experience.
options.feedback: The agents aren't dishonest — they're rational optimizers doing exactly what the incentive structure rewards. Punishing them without changing the metric just creates fear. The system created the behavior. Fix the system.

options.text: The metric is being gamed because it's a proxy (speed) for the actual goal (resolution). Add a counter-metric like customer satisfaction or re-open rate to create tension that makes gaming harder.
options.feedback: This is Goodhart's Law in action. Resolution time was a proxy for customer satisfaction. Once it became a target, agents optimized for speed at the expense of actual resolution. Adding CSAT or re-open rate as a paired metric creates productive tension — you can't game speed without hurting satisfaction.

options.text: The metric needs to be recalibrated — perhaps measure 'time to first resolution' instead of 'time to ticket close' to account for premature closures.
options.feedback: This is a marginal improvement but misses the deeper point: any single metric, once targeted, will be gamed. A new single metric will get gamed in a new way. The solution isn't a better proxy — it's multiple metrics in tension, or measuring the actual outcome (customer satisfaction).

challenges.situation: Delhi's cobra bounty program: citizens started breeding cobras to collect bounties. When the program was cancelled, they released the cobras, making the population larger than before.
challenges.question: What fundamental error did the British administrators make?

options.text: They underestimated human dishonesty — they didn't anticipate that people would cheat a system designed to help the community.
options.feedback: The cobra breeders weren't dishonest — they were rational. The bounty rewarded dead cobras, and breeding was the most efficient way to produce dead cobras. They optimized for exactly what was measured. The error wasn't trusting people too much. It was designing an incentive that diverged from the goal.

options.text: They confused the metric (dead cobras delivered) with the outcome (fewer living cobras), creating an incentive that could be optimized without achieving the actual goal.
options.feedback: The metric was a proxy for the goal. Once rewarded, rational actors found the easiest path to the metric (breeding) rather than the easiest path to the goal (reducing wild population). This is universal: attach a reward to a measurement, and you create measurement-optimizers, not outcome-producers.

options.text: They failed to enforce the program properly — with better monitoring of where the cobras came from, they could have prevented the gaming.
options.feedback: Adding enforcement is a band-aid that creates an arms race. The deeper lesson is structural: any single metric, once targeted, creates a game. The answer isn't policing the game better — it's designing incentives that make the easy path and the right path the same path.

challenges.front: If our current metrics are being gamed, we just need better, more precise metrics that can't be manipulated.
challenges.back: There is no ungameable single metric. Goodhart's Law and Campbell's Law are properties of measurement itself, not failures of specific measures. Every metric, once targeted, creates a new optimization game. The solution isn't precision — it's structural: multiple metrics in tension, rotating targets, direct observation of outcomes, and spending time in the reality the numbers claim to represent.

challenges.context: Two engineering managers want to improve developer productivity on their teams.

scenarioA.label: Single metric target
scenarioA.text: Implements 'pull requests merged per week' as the team metric, displayed on a dashboard. Within a month, PRs get smaller and more trivial. Developers split features into 8 tiny PRs instead of 2 meaningful ones. The dashboard looks amazing. Actual feature delivery hasn't changed. The team has created PR-optimizers, not productivity.

scenarioB.label: Metrics in tension
scenarioB.text: Tracks PR throughput AND deployment frequency AND change failure rate together. Gaming one metric necessarily hurts another. Splitting into trivial PRs improves throughput but doesn't improve deployment frequency. Rushing hurts change failure rate. The tension between metrics makes genuine performance the easiest path.

challenges.explanation: A single metric creates a single game to optimize. Multiple metrics in tension make gaming harder because optimizing one at the expense of another is visible. The DORA metrics work precisely because they pull against each other — speed vs. stability. When genuine performance is the easiest way to satisfy all metrics simultaneously, gaming becomes harder than doing the actual work.

challenges.instruction: Order these strategies from most vulnerable to least vulnerable to metric gaming:
items: Single metric tied to individual bonuses
Multiple metrics reported but only one rewarded
Paired metrics in tension with each other
Direct observation of outcomes + rotating metric focus
correctOrder: Single metric tied to individual bonuses
Multiple metrics reported but only one rewarded
Paired metrics in tension with each other
Direct observation of outcomes + rotating metric focus

principle.text: When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.
principle.subtext: People don't game metrics because they're dishonest — they game them because they're rational. Design systems where the easy path and the right path are the same path.

reflection: What metric in your life or work has become a target that you optimize for at the expense of the actual goal? What would change if you measured the outcome directly instead of the proxy?
